figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison of agent paradigms. We contrast five approaches to constructing AI agents for solving tasks. Single Agent [Yao et al., 2023] uses one LLM that reasons and acts in a loop until completion. Hyperagent [Zhang et al., 2026b] augments a single agent with recursive self-evolution, iteratively refining its behavior across turns. Multi Agent [Hong et al., 2024] decomposes tasks via a planner that assigns subtasks to specialized workers, which coordinate and merge outputs. OpenClaw [Steinberger, 2026] is a personal assistant that learns online from interaction signals (user replies, tool outputs), with persistent memory and tool search. Meta Agent (ours) introduces a meta-level agent that constructs a task-specific multi-agent system at inference time: it both creates worker agents (solid arrows) and verifies them (dashed arrows). Unlike prior paradigms, it treats the agent system itself as a synthesize-and-validate artifact per task.

Table 1: Comparison of agent paradigms. Prior work operates at different levels of agent design, ranging from fixed reasoning loops (ReAct), self-evolving single agents (HyperAgent), predefined multi-agent workflows (MetaGPT, AutoGen), and lifelong adaptive agents (OpenClaw). In contrast, Meta-Agent constructs a fully executable multi-agent system from task descriptions, explicitly synthesizing agent structure and enforcing unified verification across both construction and execution stages.
Table 2: Comparison against baselines on six benchmarks. All methods use GPT-4o-mini and the test splits established by Zhang et al. [2025d]. Bold marks the best score in each column.

Figure 2: Overview of the Meta-Agent. Phase 1 (left) transforms a task description into a DAG of verified agents through a pipeline consisting of prompt analysis, architecture planning, API research, code generation, and verification. Verification failures are typed and routed back to the responsible upstream stage. Phase 2 (right) executes the resulting multi-agent DAG under continuous verification. A coordinator dispatches agents, an in-memory context store routes intermediate outputs, and typed error attribution distinguishes local, upstream, and structural failures to determine the corrective action.
